q: Sense organs convert the stimulus into electrical signals or action potential which are then sent to the specific region of the brain.
Sclera is the outermost coat of the eyeball, which is tough, fibrous and opaque, and maintains the shape of the eyeball.
Cornea is a transparent layer continuous with the sclera that refracts the light rays that enter the eye.
Conjunctiva is a transparent membrane that covers the exposed part of the eyeball and protects the cornea.
Choroid contains a black pigment that absorbs light and prevents internal reflection of light.
The ciliary body is a thickened region at the front end of the choroid that changes the thickness of the lens during accommodation.
The iris contains circular muscles and radial muscles that change the size of the pupil through antagonistic action.
Retina consists of two types of photoreceptor cells: cone cells and rod cells.
Rod cells are sensitive under low light and are responsible for vision at night.
Cone cells are responsible for colour vision under conditions of high light intensity.
The Fovea (Yellow spot) is situated in line with the optical axis of the lens and provides the clearest vision.
The blind spot is the area where neurones leave the retina and form the optic nerve, and it has no photoreceptors.
Aqueous humour maintains the internal pressure of the eye and provides nutrients for the lens and cornea.
Vitreous humour is a jelly-like substance that prevents collapse of the eye and refracts light rays.
Stereoscopic vision allows a person to judge the distance and depth of objects and to estimate the speed of moving objects more accurately.
